title: Whittled ash marking gauge
subtitle: A functional mortise-marking gauge cut from seasoned ash with a sloyd knife and brace-and-bit
excerpt: A marking gauge is the joinery tool that scribes a line parallel to an edge. Making one in seasoned ash puts a standard workshop tool in the maker's kit and teaches mortise-and-tenon proportions in the process. The stem is whittled to section, the stock is bored with a brace-and-bit, and the wedge-and-pin assembly is carved to fit. No lathe, no machine tools.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the marking-gauge stock and stem proportions. Newey & Drage, Practical Carpentry and Joinery (1898) for the pin-and-wedge assembly method.

glossaryTerms.definition: Timber dried to a stable moisture content, typically 8 to 12 per cent for indoor tool use. Seasoned ash is dense, elastic, and resistant to splitting.
The sawn piece of timber from which a component is cut or shaped.

content.text: A marking gauge has three parts: a stem (the long rod the pin sits in), a stock (the sliding block that runs along the edge of the work), and a wedge (the locking piece that fixes the stock at the set distance). Making one in
seasoned ash

teaches proportioned joinery work and produces a gauge that is better in the hand than a plastic-body equivalent. Ash is the right material: its elasticity allows the wedge to lock without splitting the stock, and its density keeps the stem from flexing. Beech is the traditional alternative; oak works but is harder to bore and carve.
What you need: wood
For the stem: a
blank

of seasoned ash 22 cm long, 16 mm square. For the stock: a block 8 cm long, 4 cm wide, 3 cm deep. For the wedge: a short piece 6 cm long, 2 cm wide, 8 mm at the thick end tapering to 3 mm. All from the same board for consistent grain movement. Moisture content at or below 12 per cent; a moisture meter check is reliable.
What you need: tools

A sloyd knife for the stem section and wedge. A brace-and-bit to bore the stem mortise through the stock. A bench chisel to square the mortise walls. A tenon saw to cut the wedge slot. A try square to mark out the mortise. A leather strop. Boiled linseed oil for the finish.
Safety
Clamp the stock block in a vice for all boring and chisel work. The free hand must be behind the chisel cutting edge at all times.
The work
Step 1: Shape the stem to section

The stem is rectangular in section: 14 mm by 12 mm. Take push cuts along the four long faces of the square blank with the sloyd knife to remove the saw marks and bring the section to a consistent 14 mm by 12 mm along the full 22 cm length. Keep the faces flat; use the try square to check that adjacent faces are perpendicular. A consistent rectangular stem slides smoothly through the stock mortise.
Step 2: Bore the mortise in the stock

Mark the mortise on the stock block: centred on the 4 cm face, 14 mm wide and 12 mm tall. Bore a series of overlapping holes through the 3 cm depth with the brace-and-bit at a diameter just under the mortise width, then pare back to the marked line with the bench chisel. Work from both faces to avoid tear-out at the exit. The mortise must be straight and perpendicular; check with the try square after each paring pass.
Step 3: Cut the wedge slot in the stock

The wedge slot runs from the top face of the stock down to the mortise. Mark a slot 5 mm wide, centred on the mortise width, running 15 mm deep from the top face. Cut with the tenon saw and clear with the bench chisel. The wedge will be inserted here and driven down to lock the stem.
Step 4: Fit the stem and wedge

Slide the stem through the stock mortise. It should slide without binding but without lateral play. Carve the wedge from the small blank: it tapers over 6 cm from 8 mm thick at the top to 3 mm at the tip. The wedge taper must match the taper of the slot; try it in place and pare the thick end until it locks the stem firmly when tapped down by hand.
Step 5: Fit the pin

The scribing pin is a cut nail or a small steel pin, 1.5 mm in diameter. Bore a 1.5 mm pilot hole through the stem face at the working end, 5 mm from the tip. Drive the pin through until 2 to 3 mm protrudes from the lower face. File the pin to a point if needed.
Finishing

A light 180-grit sand removes tool marks. Apply boiled linseed oil to all surfaces, leave thirty minutes, wipe off. Two coats, 24 hours apart. Boiled linseed oil seals the timber and keeps it from picking up the moisture that would swell the stock and bind the stem.
Variations
A mortise gauge has two pins: fit a second pin in a slot parallel to the first, adjustable by a small threaded rod or a wedge of its own. The additional machining is a separate session.
The stock can be shaped with chamfers along its long edges: two push cuts at forty-five degrees on each long corner give a more traditional appearance and are more comfortable in the hand.
Care
Store away from moisture and direct heat. Re-oil once a year with a light wipe of boiled linseed. The stem and stock will wear to a close fit with use and need no further adjustment.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Problems in a hand-made marking gauge.

items.symptom: The stem binds in the stock mortise
items.cause: The mortise walls are not perpendicular, or the stem section is slightly too large for the mortise.
items.fix: Check the stem dimensions against the mortise with callipers. Pare the mortise walls with a bench chisel on the tight side; take thin passes and test-fit after each pass.

items.symptom: The wedge does not lock the stem firmly
items.cause: The wedge taper does not match the slot taper, so the wedge bottoms out in the slot without gripping the stem.
items.fix: Remove the wedge and pare the thicker end until the wedge grips the stem at about two-thirds of its depth in the slot.
